properties.description: Confidential clerk at Tellson's Bank
Odd-job man and messenger for Tellson's Bank
Lucie is Dr. Manette's daughter, raised believing he was dead
Dr. Manette is Lucie's father, imprisoned since she was an infant
Defarge was formerly Dr. Manette's servant and now cares for him in the garret
Ernest and Therese Defarge are married and run the wine-shop together
Madame Defarge is Ernest's wife, ever-knitting and watchful
Miss Pross is Lucie's fiercely devoted companion and protector
Lorry carried Lucie to England as an infant and serves as her connection to Tellson's Bank
Imprisoned for eighteen years in cell 105, North Tower
Kept in a locked garret above Defarge's wine-shop after release from the Bastille
Proprietor of the wine-shop in Saint Antoine
Sits behind the counter knitting
The wine-shop is in the Saint Antoine quarter
The garret is on the fifth floor above the wine-shop

properties.role: recipient
properties.description: Receives the message and replies 'Recalled to Life'

properties.role: messenger
properties.description: Delivers the dispatch from Tellson's Bank

properties.role: revealer
properties.description: Tells Lucie her father is alive

properties.role: recipient
properties.description: Learns her father is alive and faints from shock

properties.role: caretaker
properties.description: Rushes in to tend to the fainted Lucie

properties.role: instigator
properties.description: Writes 'BLOOD' on the wall with wine-lees

properties.role: observer
properties.description: Watches from his shop and rebukes Gaspard for writing BLOOD

properties.role: daughter
properties.description: Reunites with her imprisoned father for the first time

properties.role: escort
properties.description: Accompanies Lucie to identify Dr. Manette

properties.role: guide
properties.description: Leads them to the garret and unlocks the door

properties.role: subject
properties.description: Found making shoes, identifies himself only by his cell number

properties.role: organizer
properties.description: Arranges the carriage and papers for departure

properties.role: companion
properties.description: Holds her father's arm as they leave

properties.role: rescued
properties.description: Leaves Paris clutching his shoemaking tools

properties.role: facilitator
properties.description: Drives the coach and handles papers at the city gate
Darnay loves Lucie from the hour of his trial and marries her after gaining Dr. Manette's consent
Darnay is the nephew of the Marquis, who is his father's twin brother and inheritor of the Evrémonde estate
Darnay marries Lucie and promises to reveal his true name (Evrémonde) on the wedding morning
Carton confesses his hopeless love to Lucie and pledges to sacrifice anything for her and those dear to her
Carton bears a striking resemblance to Darnay, which saves Darnay at trial; they later form an uneasy friendship
Carton does all intellectual legal work for Stryver while Stryver takes the credit and fame; they were schoolmates at Shrewsbury
Partners in running the wine-shop and leading the revolutionary Jacques network
Defarge was Dr. Manette's servant before his imprisonment and cared for him when he was released
Jerry abuses his wife for praying, believing her prayers undermine his grave-robbing trade
Young Jerry is his express image, serves as his stand-in at Tellson's, and aspires to follow in his resurrection man trade
Jerry serves as Mr. Lorry's bodyguard and messenger, accompanying him to Paris
Miss Pross has cared for Lucie since she was ten years old with fierce, selfless devotion
Miss Pross maintains deluded faith in her brother Solomon despite his having stripped her of everything
Barsad and Cly work together to frame Darnay for treason, having known each other seven or eight years
Gaspard murders the Marquis to avenge his child who was killed by the Marquis's carriage
Gabelle manages the Evrémonde estate on Darnay's instructions to spare the people, and is imprisoned for serving an emigrant
Little Lucie is the daughter of Charles and Lucie Darnay
Little Lucie is the daughter of Lucie, chattering in the tongues of the Two Cities
The Vengeance serves as Madame Defarge's chief lieutenant among the women of Saint Antoine
Defarge brings the mender of roads to Paris to testify before the Jacques about Gaspard
Stryver intends to propose to Lucie but is dissuaded by Mr. Lorry before he can do so
Jerry serves as odd-job man and porter outside Tellson's Bank
Mr. Lorry works as a clerk at Tellson's Bank for over sixty years
The Doctor lives and practices medicine at the quiet corner in Soho
Lucie creates a happy home at the Soho lodgings with her father and later with Darnay
Defarge owns and operates the wine-shop in Saint Antoine
Madame Defarge presides at the counter of the wine-shop, perpetually knitting her register
The Marquis lives at the chateau and is murdered there in his bed
Gabelle serves as postmaster and tax functionary of the village
The mender of roads works on the hillside above the village
Carton works through the nights at Stryver's chambers preparing legal briefs
Jerry and his family live in Hanging-sword-alley, Whitefriars
Gabelle is imprisoned in the Abbaye and writes his desperate letter to Darnay from there

properties.role: the accused prisoner on trial for treason
barrister whose resemblance to Darnay destroys the prosecution's identification
reluctant witness for the prosecution
witness who cannot identify Darnay due to his mental state during the voyage
witness who testifies about the Dover mail journey
chief prosecution witness, exposed as spy and scoundrel
prosecution witness posing as Darnay's honest servant
defense counsel for Darnay
messenger for Mr. Lorry, carries the acquittal verdict back to Tellson's
the aristocrat whose carriage kills the child
the father of the killed child
comforts Gaspard and is identified by the Marquis
stands watching steadily and knitting as the scene unfolds
the assassin who stabs the Marquis in the heart
the victim, murdered in his bed
confronts his uncle and renounces the family property
refuses to change and vows to perpetuate the system of oppression
asks Dr. Manette for permission to court Lucie
gives conditional consent while struggling with inner turmoil
the would-be suitor dissuaded from proposing
tactfully warns Stryver that his proposal would fail
confesses his love and pledges to sacrifice anything for Lucie
receives Carton's confession with compassion and promises to keep his secret
leads the grave-robbing expedition to dig up Roger Cly's coffin
secretly follows his father and witnesses the grave-robbing
witness to Gaspard's capture and execution
the prisoner who is captured and hanged
presides over the tribunal and declares extermination of the Evrémondes
spy attempting to gather intelligence from the Defarges
identifies Barsad and knits his name into the register
the groom who reveals his true name on the wedding morning
the bride
gives his daughter away, turns deadly pale upon hearing Darnay's true name
relapses into shoemaking for nine days after learning Darnay's true name
watches over the Doctor and maintains the secret from Lucie
discovers the relapse and helps maintain the deception
hacks the bench to pieces and burns the remains
assists Mr. Lorry by holding the candle during the destruction
asks to be accepted as a friend who may come and go freely
leads the assault and searches cell 105 North Tower
leads the women, beheads the governor with her knife
accompanies Defarge to cell 105 North Tower
seizes Foulon and releases him to the mob at the lamp-post
rouses the women and leads them in the pursuit of Foulon
the victim, hanged with grass in his mouth
barricades himself on his rooftop as the village threatens him
receives Gabelle's letter and resolves to go to France
writes the desperate letter from the Abbaye prison

properties.description: The treason trial takes place at the Old Bailey courthouse
The Marquis's carriage kills the child in the streets near a fountain in Saint Antoine
The Marquis is murdered in his bed at the chateau
Darnay confronts the Marquis over dinner at the chateau
Darnay speaks to Dr. Manette at his lodgings in Soho
Carton confesses to Lucie at the Manette home in Soho
Stryver is dissuaded from proposing during a visit to Tellson's
The Jacques meet in the garret above the wine-shop to register the Evrémondes
Barsad visits the wine-shop to spy on the Defarges
The people of Saint Antoine storm the Bastille fortress
Four revolutionaries set fire to the chateau, burning it completely
Gaspard is captured, imprisoned, and hanged in the village
Darnay receives Gabelle's letter at Tellson's Bank
The wedding party gathers at the Soho lodgings before going to a nearby church
Dr. Manette's nine-day relapse occurs at his lodgings in Soho
The treason charge against Darnay
Lucie described as the golden thread
Carton's self-lacerating mirror soliloquy
Carton's mirage of a better life
Narrator's summary of Carton's wasted potential
Carton's confession to Lucie
Carton's pledge of sacrifice
Carton's farewell words foreshadowing his sacrifice
The Marquis's philosophy of repression
Darnay renouncing the estate
Gaspard's cry at his child's death
The note on the knife driven into the Marquis's heart
Defarge explaining the knitted register
Madame Defarge on vengeance requiring time
Madame Defarge's earthquake metaphor
Madame Defarge's cold pronouncement on Darnay's fate
Dr. Manette speaking of the moon from his prison
Miss Pross discovering the relapse
Dr. Manette consenting to destroy the bench
Lucie defending Carton
Lucie urging compassion for Carton
Defarge finding Manette's inscription in the Bastille
Foulon being hanged by the mob
Gabelle's letter revealing Darnay's true identity as Marquis St. Evrémonde
Darnay departing for France
Opening description of Tellson's Bank
The storming of the Bastille
The burning of the chateau
Miss Pross praising her worthless brother Solomon
Carton's prophetic remark about the crowd
The narrator on Carton's relationship with the Darnay children
Darnay's declaration of love to Dr. Manette
Dr. Manette asking Darnay to delay revealing his name
Darnay is imprisoned in La Force for over a year, first in secret (solitary), then among the general prisoners
Darnay is transferred to the Conciergerie before each of his two trials
Darnay travels to Paris to save Gabelle, encountering hostile patriots along the way
Darnay is tried and acquitted at his first trial before the Revolutionary Tribunal
Darnay is unanimously condemned to death at his second trial based on Manette's letter
Carton drugs Darnay and switches clothes with him to take his place at the guillotine
Carton goes to the guillotine as number Twenty-Three, dying in Darnay's place
Carton threatens Barsad with denunciation to gain access to Darnay in the Conciergerie
Carton walks Paris all night repeating scripture, finding peace before his sacrifice
Carton bears a striking resemblance to Darnay and sacrifices his life to save him by switching places in the Conciergerie
Carton comforts the seamstress in their final moments, holding her hand in the tumbril and at the scaffold
Manette uses his Bastille prisoner status to save Darnay from the September Massacres
Manette's hidden letter from the Bastille, denouncing the Evrémondes, is used to condemn his own son-in-law
After failing to save Darnay, Manette relapses into his shoemaking madness
Manette's testimony and popularity are decisive in Darnay's acquittal at the first trial
Manette is Darnay's father-in-law; he saves him from the massacres but his letter inadvertently condemns him
Lucie stands for two hours daily near La Force prison for over a year
Lucie pleads with Madame Defarge for mercy toward her husband
Lucie flees Paris in the coach with her family while Carton takes her husband's place
Defarge conducts Darnay to La Force prison and refuses to help him
Defarge found Manette's letter in the Bastille chimney and produces it at the trial
Defarge is one of the three who denounce Darnay to the Section of Saint Antoine
Madame Defarge coldly studies Lucie and her child, memorizing their faces for future denunciation
Madame Defarge is one of the three who denounce Darnay
Madame Defarge is killed when her own pistol discharges during her struggle with Miss Pross
Madame Defarge reveals she is the surviving younger sister of the peasant family destroyed by the Evrémondes
Miss Pross physically fights Madame Defarge, killing her and losing her hearing in the process
Barsad is blackmailed by Carton into granting access to Darnay in the Conciergerie
Barsad facilitates Carton's entry into Darnay's cell and carries the drugged Darnay out
Barsad is Miss Pross's brother Solomon, long estranged from her
Cruncher reveals that Roger Cly's burial was faked, providing Carton additional leverage over Barsad
Lorry organizes the escape, prepares the coach, presents papers at the barrier, and drives the party to safety
Lorry occupies rooms in the Paris branch of Tellson's Bank during the Revolution
The Vengeance accompanies Madame Defarge to observe Lucie's family
Jacques Three serves on the jury that condemns Darnay, with bloodthirsty enthusiasm
The wood-sawyer watches Lucie during her daily vigil and is rehearsed by the Defarges to testify she made signals to prisoners
Gabelle testifies at Darnay's first trial, confirming the letter that brought Darnay to France
Little Lucie escapes Paris in the coach with her mother and grandfather
The grindstone scene of the September Massacres takes place in the courtyard of Tellson's Paris branch
Darnay's first trial and acquittal occurs at the Revolutionary Tribunal
Darnay's second trial and condemnation occurs at the Revolutionary Tribunal
Carton's substitution for Darnay takes place in a cell of the Conciergerie
The fatal confrontation between Miss Pross and Madame Defarge occurs in Lucie's deserted Paris lodging
Carton is executed at the Place of Execution where the guillotine stands
The letter was written in and hidden in the chimney of cell One Hundred and Five, North Tower of the Bastille
The Carmagnole dance swirls around Lucie near the wood-sawyer's shop by La Force prison
The escape coach must pass through the Paris barrier where papers are examined
The younger Evrémonde twin destroyed Madame Defarge's family -- her sister, brother, brother-in-law, father, and unborn niece/nephew
Darnay's mother visited Doctor Manette seeking to make amends for the Evrémonde crimes and charged young Charles to be faithful in making reparations
She was the wife of the younger Evrémonde twin, unhappy in her marriage and fearful of both her husband and his brother
The elder Marquis arranged Manette's imprisonment in the Bastille after intercepting his letter to the Minister about the Evrémonde crimes
Darnay is the son of the younger Evrémonde twin, as revealed by his mother's visit to Manette with the young boy Charles
Darnay encounters a hostile mob at Beauvais during his journey to Paris
Madame Defarge reveals her identity in the wine-shop, overheard by Carton
Cly and Barsad were partners; Barsad helped stage Cly's fake burial in London
Darnay's imprisonment occurs in Chapter 1 of Book 3
The grindstone scene occurs in Chapter 2 of Book 3
Darnay's acquittal occurs in Chapter 6 of Book 3
Darnay's re-arrest occurs in Chapter 7 of Book 3
Darnay's condemnation occurs at the end of Chapter 10 of Book 3
Carton's substitution occurs in Chapter 13 of Book 3
Miss Pross's fight with Madame Defarge occurs in Chapter 14 of Book 3
Carton's execution occurs in Chapter 15 of Book 3
The novel's most famous line is part of Carton's imagined final thoughts at the guillotine
Manette's denunciation concludes the Bastille letter that condemns Darnay
Madame Defarge's speech revealing her identity as the surviving sister
Carton sacrifices his life for Darnay, achieving spiritual resurrection through selfless love
Carton is 'recalled to life' spiritually through his sacrifice -- 'It is a far, far better thing that I do'
Carton is Darnay's physical double but his moral opposite -- the dissolute man who becomes the novel's greatest hero
Manette is literally 'recalled to life' after eighteen years buried alive in the Bastille
Manette's physical imprisonment becomes psychological -- he relapses to shoemaking under stress even after release
Lucie devotes herself entirely to restoring her father and supporting her husband, the 'golden thread' binding everyone
Madame Defarge represents the revolution's consuming vengeance, knitting the names of the condemned into her register
Her personal vendetta against the Evremonde family drives the prosecution, blurring justice and revenge
Darnay lives a double identity -- French aristocrat by birth, English gentleman by choice -- torn between two cities
Darnay is condemned for the sins of his family despite renouncing them, showing how revolutionary justice consumes the innocent
Lorry straddles two worlds as a man who claims to be 'a mere machine of business' but repeatedly shows deep compassion
Miss Pross sacrifices her hearing in the confrontation with Madame Defarge, defending Lucie with fierce loyalty
